Read through the commitment-problem lens of this paper, that reads as a rules-based exit from recurring bilateral crises rather than a concession of leverage, predictable for the islands and bounded for the Netherlands, and it is the proposal a Kingdom partner can most readily co-sign.

A second Kingdom move addresses the open parametric layer rather than the foreclosed credit one, and it reaches the islands that CCRIF membership does not currently cover. What a single small island lacks is the scale to pool its risk and the standing to buy reinsurance on good terms, which is exactly what a regional facility supplies to its members. The Netherlands could supply it directly, standing up a Kingdom facility that aggregates the islands' exposure, buys parametric reinsurance in the international market on their behalf, and passes tailored cover down to the ABC islands and the Caribbean Netherlands. The design that preserves this paper's logic keeps the risk transferred onward to the reinsurance market rather than held on the Dutch balance sheet, because a facility that carried the risk itself would rebuild the sole-provider dependency the pre-arranged instruments are meant to dissolve. That same feature meets the objection a Dutch reader raises first. A purchasing-and-pooling facility asks the Netherlands to lend its scale and standing, not its balance sheet, and it neither refinances island debt nor guarantees it, so it does not raise the moral-hazard concern that borrowing through the Kingdom is read as raising, and it creates no new lever of conditionality in either direction. For the Caribbean Netherlands the case is not discretionary: the Algemene Rekenkamer has already found that the public bodies hold no safety net [@rekenkamer2026vangnet], so arranging parametric cover there discharges a responsibility the Netherlands already carries, while for the autonomous countries the same scheme is a product they opt into on their own terms. The mechanics resemble the regional pools already operating, and the lowest-cost route may be to buy into that machinery rather than rebuild it, but the principle holds: the Kingdom can be the aggregator that scale would otherwise deny each island on its own.

# Limitations

The paper as drafted is a v0.3 working paper. The reserve-holdings comparison across the five jurisdictions is pulled directly from the most recent IMF Article IV consultations and forms the empirical spine of Sections 2 and 5. The toolkit description in Section 3 rests on public instrument documentation and on the co-authored World Bank assessment and public options paper for Sint Maarten [@boyer2023sxmdrf; @wb2024sxmoptions]. The layer-by-layer reading of the Kingdom constraint in Section 4 rests on those same primary sources for the eligibility facts and on the Aruban Court of Audit for the public-private precedent [@rekenkameraruba2018]. The commitment-problem scaffolding in Section 6 rests on the disaster risk finance literature anchored in Clarke and Dercon [@clarke2016dull; @clarkewrenlewis2016]. Several classes of further work remain open before a v1.0 release, and we name them so the reader can calibrate the claims.

The legal-framework review is asserted at the level of institutional pattern for the central bank statutes, though the Kingdom-level eligibility and borrowing constraints are now sourced to primary documents. A line-by-line review of the CBA and CBCS statutes against the analogous clauses in the ECCB Agreement and the Central Bank of Barbados Act would refine the claim that, for the open layers, the binding constraint is institutional rather than statutory. We expect the review to refine rather than overturn the claim. The accounting-standard question of how a supervisor should treat an off-balance-sheet corporatized vehicle, whether by an ESA-style risk-and-reward test or otherwise, is raised in the Aruban material but not yet pinned to a named standard, and the relevant supervisor advice letters are the place to settle it.

The institutional-pathway analysis is developed at the level of pattern in the public record, and it is offered as a reading open to correction rather than as a settled account of anyone's reasoning. We read the silence in the Aruba and CSM Article IVs as silence, not as evidence of a particular deliberation, and what lies behind the pattern is exactly what documents alone cannot establish. The natural next step is direct engagement with current and former CBCS, CBA and ECCB leadership, and the posture here is an open door rather than a completed interview programme: we would welcome exchange that tests, corrects or deepens the reading, and we publish in the working-paper spirit precisely to invite it from the institutions the paper discusses. The version offered here is the inferential reconstruction the record supports, strengthened by the Article IV asymmetry and by the Sint Maarten technical-cooperation record which shows the absence to be a surveillance-conversation absence rather than a pipeline one [@boyer2023sxmdrf]. A data limit also stands: the granular per-member CCRIF coverage record is not publicly disclosed at the member level, so the coverage detail the paper uses is limited to public disclosures. The reconciliation of the two Sint Maarten average-annual-loss figures is resolved here, the government-liability figure of US\$5.8m from the CCRIF SPC 2019 model and the whole-economy hurricane figure of US\$42.3m are different scopes of the same risk rather than competing estimates [@wb2024sxmoptions], and the paper uses the government-liability figure where the argument concerns the fund's remit.

We make the v0.3 working paper public because the empirical spine is in hand, because the toolkit and the layer-by-layer constraint are now sourced to primary documents, because the corporatization mechanism is anchored in a co-authored assessment and a supreme-audit-institution report, and because the framing correctly reads a shifting landscape in which the fiscal-resilience register is the durable one. Publishing openly is itself part of the method rather than a step that precedes it. A working paper placed into the conversation invites the institutions it discusses to respond on their own terms, and those responses, alongside the line-by-line statutory review still to come, are what a v1.0 will be built on. The argument is the same. The evidence will be deeper.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
